title: Watch Your Money
description: Taxes, accounting, and the banking deep dive. Federal and state tax obligations, sales tax, bookkeeping expectations, and the detailed reality of opening and maintaining a U.S. bank account as a foreign founder.

lessons.title: Federal and State Taxes — What You Actually Owe
objectives: Distinguish between federal and Florida state tax obligations for foreign-owned entities
Identify key IRS filing requirements including ITIN vs. SSN for foreign founders
Explain how entity type affects your tax treatment (pass-through vs. corporate)

contentBlocks.title: Welcome to the Financial District
contentBlocks.content:
You have set up your entity and picked your business structure back in the city. Now the road leads into the Financial District, a corridor of accounting firms, tax offices, and bank branches where the real money questions get answered.

What would you do if the IRS sent you a $1,200 penalty before you even made a profit? A founder from Brazil set up an LLC in Orlando and assumed she only needed to file once a year. She missed quarterly estimated payments for two consecutive quarters, and the penalty was $1,200 before she even turned a profit.

contentBlocks.title: Tax Entity Comparison Calculator
contentBlocks.titleEs: Calculadora de Comparación Tributaria por Entidad
contentBlocks.description: Compare how much you'd pay in taxes as an LLC, C-Corp, or S-Corp based on your net profit.
contentBlocks.descriptionEs: Compare cuánto pagaría en impuestos como LLC, C-Corp o S-Corp según su ganancia neta.

contentBlocks.title: What You'll Actually Owe — Tax Layer Breakdown
contentBlocks.htmlContent:
What You'll Actually Owe — Tax Layer Breakdown

Representative rates for a C-Corp vs LLC in Florida

C-Corp in Florida — ~27% effective
Federal 21%
FL 5.5%
Local fees ~0.5% (varies by county)
LLC (Pass-Through) in Florida — ~27.8% effective
Self-Employ 15.3%
Federal Income 12%
Florida State Tax: 0% — No state income tax!Local fees ~0.5%
Federal / Self-EmploymentState / Federal IncomeLocal Fees
Florida advantage: No personal state income tax saves LLC owners 4–13% compared to states like NY or CA.

data.footer: Florida advantage: No personal state income tax saves LLC owners 4-13% compared to states like NY or CA.

contentBlocks.accessibilityNote: Stacked horizontal bar chart comparing tax layers for C-Corp versus LLC in Florida. C-Corp shows Federal Corporate Tax at 21%, Florida Corporate Tax at 5.5%, and Local Fees at 0.5% for approximately 27% effective rate. LLC shows Self-Employment Tax at 15.3%, Federal Income Tax at 12%, Florida State Tax at 0%, and Local Fees at 0.5% for approximately 27.8% effective rate. Key takeaway: Florida has no personal state income tax, saving LLC owners 4 to 13 percent compared to states like New York or California.

contentBlocks.title: Florida Has No Income Tax — But That Does Not Mean No Taxes
contentBlocks.content:
Here is one of the biggest reasons international founders choose Florida: there is no state personal income tax. That is real money staying in your pocket. Florida is one of only nine U.S. states with no personal income tax, and the Orlando metro area consistently ranks among the top regions in the Southeast for small business growth.

But no state income tax does not mean no taxes. Federal taxes still apply to every U.S. business, and the IRS will want to hear from you regardless of where you incorporated or how much revenue you earned.

Important: Tax obligations vary based on your entity type, revenue, and personal tax residency status. The information here is educational. Consult a qualified CPA or tax attorney for your specific situation.

contentBlocks.title: Federal vs. State vs. Local — What Applies to You

rows: Income tax, self-employment tax, payroll tax
Entity type determines how you file. LLCs pass through to personal returns. C-Corps file separately.
No personal income tax; 5.5% corporate income tax for C-Corps only
LLCs and S-Corps avoid Florida corporate income tax because income passes through to owners.
Business licenses, tangible personal property tax
Orange County requires a business tax receipt. Tangible property tax applies to equipment and furniture.

contentBlocks.title: ITIN, SSN, and Why Your Tax ID Matters
contentBlocks.content:
Think of your tax ID as the key to every building in the Financial District. Before you can file anything with the IRS, you need a tax identification number. Which one depends on your immigration status.

The bottom line? An SSN (Social Security Number) is issued to people authorized to work in the U.S. If you have a valid work visa (E-2, O-1, L-1, or H-1B), you can apply for an SSN. This is the preferred number because it connects to the broader credit and financial system.

An ITIN (Individual Taxpayer Identification Number) is for non-residents who have U.S. tax obligations but don't qualify for an SSN. You apply with IRS Form W-7, and the process takes 7 to 11 weeks.

The practical problem: some banks won't open accounts without an SSN, and if you apply for an ITIN by mail, you may need to send your original passport to the IRS. A Colombian founder was without his passport for nine weeks while the IRS processed his application.

The workaround: Apply in person at an IRS Taxpayer Assistance Center. They verify your passport on the spot and you keep it. The nearest center to Orlando is in Tampa — about two hours round trip, but you avoid mailing your passport.

contentBlocks.title: How Entity Type Affects Your Tax Treatment
contentBlocks.content:
Now that you know the lay of the Financial District, step inside the tax office. Your entity type decides how the IRS treats your income, what forms you file, and how much you owe. That's one of the main reasons picking the right entity matters (covered in Module 1).

Let's put this in perspective. LLC (taxed as sole proprietorship or partnership): Income "passes through" to your personal tax return. You pay federal income tax at your personal rate, plus self-employment tax of 15.3% on net earnings. You file Schedule C (single-member) or Form 1065 (multi-member) with your personal Form 1040-NR.

C-Corporation: Files its own return (Form 1120) and pays corporate income tax at a flat 21% rate. If you pay yourself a salary or dividends, you also pay personal income tax on that income — "double taxation." The upside: C-Corps are the preferred structure for raising investment capital.

S-Corporation: Similar pass-through treatment as an LLC, but with potential self-employment tax savings. The catch: non-resident aliens cannot be S-Corp shareholders. No green card or citizenship means S-Corp is off the table.

A German tech founder chose a C-Corp to raise venture capital. A Mexican food service founder chose an LLC for simple pass-through taxation. The wrong choice would have cost each of them thousands in unnecessary taxes or restructuring fees.

contentBlocks.title: Tax Breakdown by Jurisdiction — A Closer Look

tabs.label: Federal (IRS)
tabs.content:
**What you owe:** Corporate income tax (C-Corp: 21% flat rate) or pass-through income tax (LLC/S-Corp: reported on personal return).

**Key forms:** Form 1120 (C-Corp), Form 1065 (Partnership), Schedule C (Sole Proprietor).

**Estimated payments:** Quarterly (April 15, June 15, September 15, January 15).

**International twist:** ITIN required if you don't qualify for SSN. Treaty benefits may reduce withholding.

tabs.label: State (Florida)
tabs.content:
**Good news:** Florida has NO personal state income tax.

**Corporate tax:** 5.5% on taxable income over $50,000 (C-Corps only).

**Sales tax:** 6% state rate + county surcharge (up to 1.5% in Orange County).

**Annual report:** $138.75 (LLC/LP) or $150 (Corp) — due by May 1 each year through SunBiz.

tabs.label: Local
tabs.content:
**Business Tax Receipt (BTR):** Required by Orange County and City of Orlando — typically $25–$100/year.

**Tourist Development Tax:** 6% on short-term rentals (if applicable).

**Impact fees:** Only for construction/development businesses.

**Bottom line:** Local obligations are minimal for most service and technology businesses.

contentBlocks.title: International founder consulting with a tax advisor about U.S. business taxes
You Missed a Quarterly Payment — Now What?
contentBlocks.description: It is July. You set up your LLC in January and have been earning revenue since March. You just realized you have not made any estimated tax payments to the IRS. What should you do?

paths.label: Wait until the annual tax filing deadline to pay everything at once
paths.outcome: Dangerous. The IRS charges penalties for each quarter you miss. Waiting until April means penalties accumulate for Q1, Q2, Q3, and Q4. The longer you wait, the more you owe in interest and penalties — exactly what happened to the Brazilian founder who got a $1,200 surprise.

paths.label: Make an estimated payment now and catch up on missed quarters
paths.outcome: Best move. Pay what you owe for Q1 and Q2 immediately, then set up quarterly payments going forward. The IRS may still charge a small penalty for the late quarters, but it will be much less than waiting. Use Form 1040-ES or pay directly at irs.gov/payments.

paths.label: Call your CPA and ask them to handle it
paths.outcome: Good instinct, but do not wait for the appointment. Make the payment now through irs.gov, then consult your CPA about the correct quarterly amounts going forward. Every day of delay adds to the penalty calculation.

contentBlocks.title: Tax Treaties, Check Before You Assume
contentBlocks.content: Think about this: your home country may have a tax treaty with the U.S. that lowers your tax rate, removes double taxation, or changes withholding rules. For example, many European and Latin American countries have treaties that affect how dividend income or business profits are taxed. Don't assume your home-country duties go away when you form a U.S. entity, and don't assume you'll be taxed twice without checking. A CPA who handles global clients can review your treaty status in a single meeting. This one talk can save you thousands.

contentBlocks.title: Common Mistakes International Founders Make
contentBlocks.content:
Notice these patterns:

  • Ignoring sales tax obligation. Many international founders assume their product or service is not subject to sales tax, or they don't realize they need to register before collecting it. Florida's sales tax applies to most tangible goods and some services. (More on this in the next lesson.)
  • Waiting too long to hire an accountant who knows global clients. A general CPA may not know about ITIN filing, tax treaty rules, or FBAR needs for foreign bank accounts. By the time founders realize they need a specialist, they've already made filing mistakes that cost money to fix.
  • Missing quarterly estimated payments. If you expect to owe $1,000 or more in federal tax, you must make quarterly estimated payments — due April 15, June 15, September 15, and January 15. Miss them and you will owe penalties and interest even if you pay everything when you file your annual return. Set calendar reminders for all four dates.

contentBlocks.title: Watch Your Money: What This Actually Costs
contentBlocks.content:
In other words, plan ahead. Tax and accounting costs you should budget for (as of early 2026):

  • CPA for annual tax prep (international clients): $500–$2,500/year depending on complexity
  • Quarterly estimated tax payments: Varies by income — plan for 25–30% of net profit set aside each quarter
  • ITIN application: Free (IRS Form W-7), but many founders pay $200–$400 for a Certified Acceptance Agent to help
  • Bookkeeping software: $20–$60/month (QuickBooks, Xero, Wave)
  • Penalty for missed quarterly payment: ~4–8% annualized interest on the underpayment
  • FBAR filing (if you have foreign accounts over $10,000): Typically included in CPA fee, but penalties for not filing can exceed $10,000 per account

contentBlocks.title: What Would You Do?

questions.stem: A single-member LLC in Florida earns $80,000 in net profit. Where does the owner report this income for tax purposes?
options: On the LLC's separate corporate tax return (Form 1120)
On the owner's personal tax return as pass-through income
Only on the Florida state income tax return
No filing is required because Florida has no state income tax
questions.explanation: LLC income passes through to the owner's personal tax return. The LLC itself does not file a separate corporate return (that's a C-Corp). Florida's lack of state income tax does not eliminate federal filing requirements.

questions.stem: What is the main reason an international founder without a work visa would need an ITIN?
options: To register with the Florida Department of Revenue for income tax withholding
To register their business on SunBiz
To file U.S. tax returns when they don't qualify for an SSN
To apply for a Florida driver's license
questions.explanation: An ITIN (Individual Taxpayer Identification Number) allows non-residents to meet their U.S. tax obligations when they don't qualify for a Social Security Number. It is specifically a tax filing tool issued by the IRS. Registering with the Florida Department of Revenue is a separate sales tax step that does not require an ITIN. SunBiz registration uses an EIN, not an ITIN. A Florida driver's license is unrelated to tax filing obligations.

questions.stem: A founder from Brazil operates a single-member LLC in Florida and expects to owe $8,000 in federal taxes this year. She plans to pay everything when she files her annual return in April. What problem will she face?
options: She will owe penalties and interest for missing quarterly estimated payments throughout the year
No problem — annual payment is the standard method for LLCs
Florida will charge her state income tax as a penalty for late payment
The IRS will automatically convert her LLC to a C-Corp for non-compliance
questions.explanation: The U.S. tax system requires quarterly estimated payments (April 15, June 15, September 15, January 15) if you expect to owe $1,000 or more. Missing these deadlines triggers penalties and interest even if you pay the full amount with your annual return. Annual payment is not the standard method when you owe over $1,000. Florida has no state income tax, so it cannot charge one as a penalty. The IRS does not convert entity types for non-compliance. The lesson describes a founder who owed $1,200 in penalties for this exact mistake.

contentBlocks.title: Key Takeaway
contentBlocks.content: The takeaway is clear. Florida's lack of state income tax is a real advantage, but federal obligations, quarterly estimated payments, and self-employment tax still apply to founders at every level. Budget for a CPA who works with international clients in your first year. The cost of professional help ($500–$2,500) is a fraction of the cost of penalties for filing mistakes. The Financial District has its own rules — learn them before you walk in, and they work in your favor.

lessons.title: Sales Tax, Accounting, and Keeping the Books
objectives: Explain Florida sales tax collection and remittance requirements
Describe basic U.S. bookkeeping expectations and why they differ from home-country practices
Estimate annual accounting costs for a small foreign-owned business

contentBlocks.title: Sales Tax and Bookkeeping, the Next Stop in the Financial District
contentBlocks.content:
You have left the tax office and walked down the block to the next building in the Financial District: the compliance bureau. Here is where sales tax and bookkeeping rules live, and where many founders first learn that Florida's "no income tax" reputation does not mean "no taxes."

Did you know that one founder owed $3,800 in penalties because she did not register for a free certificate before collecting sales tax? The rules are simple once you know them, but the cost of not knowing is steep.

If you sell tangible goods in Florida, you must collect sales tax. The base state rate is 6%, and most counties add a surtax of 0.5% to 1.5%. In Orange County (Orlando), the combined rate is 6.5%. In Osceola County, it is 7.5%. The rate depends on where the sale happens, not where your business is located.

Here is the practical takeaway. An Argentine founder selling leather goods from Orlando assumed sales tax only applied to big retailers. After nine months, the Florida Department of Revenue sent her a notice: $3,800 in uncollected sales tax plus penalties, paid out of pocket.

Before collecting sales tax, register for a Sales Tax Certificate with the Florida Department of Revenue : free, done online. Once registered, collect the correct rate on every taxable sale and remit to the state monthly, quarterly, or annually depending on volume.

Not everything is taxable. Most services and groceries (unprepared food) are exempt. Prepared food, clothing, electronics, and most physical products are taxable. Software and SaaS fall into a gray area — check with a CPA if you're selling digital products.

contentBlocks.title: Did You Know?
contentBlocks.content: Florida collects over $37 billion in sales and use tax revenue annually, making it the state's single largest revenue source. Because Florida has no personal income tax, sales tax carries a larger share of the state budget than in most other states. For business owners, this means sales tax compliance is not optional — it is the primary way the state funds infrastructure, education, and public services that your business depends on.

contentBlocks.title: How U.S. Sales Tax Compares to VAT — What You Already Know
contentBlocks.content:
If you're coming from almost any country outside the U.S., you're familiar with a Value Added Tax (VAT) system. In the Financial District, every building has different rules for visitors. VAT is like one central building with one set of rules. U.S. sales tax is like a district where every office makes its own — and you need a separate badge for each one.

What does that mean for you? VAT (what you know): One national rate. Applied at each stage of the supply chain. Businesses collect VAT on sales and claim credits for VAT paid on purchases. Registration thresholds and rates are set nationally.

U.S. Sales Tax (what you need to learn): No federal sales tax exists. Each state sets its own rate (or has no sales tax at all — Oregon, Montana, Delaware, New Hampshire charge zero). Counties and cities can add surtax. You only collect tax where you have "nexus" (a physical or economic connection to that state). There is no input credit system — you collect from the customer and remit to the state.

If you sell in multiple states, you register, collect, and remit in each one separately — like filing paperwork with every office in the district individually. A Colombian SaaS founder selling in Florida, Texas, and California spent $1,200 on a compliance service to handle multi-state filing.

The biggest adjustment: There is no single government portal for U.S. sales tax. You deal with each state individually. Services like Avalara or TaxJar can automate multi-state compliance. VAT is one relationship with one government. U.S. sales tax can be dozens.

contentBlocks.title: VAT vs. U.S. Sales Tax
contentBlocks.titleEs: IVA vs. Impuesto sobre Ventas en EE.UU.

rows.label: Where
rows.value: One national system — same rules everywhere in the country
rows.valueEs: Un sistema nacional — las mismas reglas en todo el país

rows.label: Applied
rows.value: At every stage of production and distribution
rows.valueEs: En cada etapa de producción y distribución

rows.label: Who Collects
rows.value: Every business in the chain collects and remits; input credits offset tax paid on purchases
rows.valueEs: Cada negocio en la cadena recauda y remite; créditos fiscales compensan el impuesto pagado en compras

rows.label: Rate
rows.value: One national rate (e.g., 21% Argentina, 19% Chile, 16% Mexico)
rows.valueEs: Una tasa nacional (ej., 21% Argentina, 19% Chile, 16% México)

rows.label: Included in Price
rows.value: Yes — the price on the shelf is the price you pay
rows.valueEs: Sí — el precio en la etiqueta es el precio que pagas

rows.label: Example
rows.value: A $100 product with 21% VAT → you pay $100 (tax already included)
rows.valueEs: Un producto de $100 con 21% IVA → pagas $100 (impuesto ya incluido)

rows.label: Where
rows.value: State by state — each state sets its own rules, rates, and exemptions
rows.valueEs: Estado por estado — cada estado establece sus propias reglas, tasas y exenciones

rows.label: Applied
rows.value: Only at the final point of sale to the consumer
rows.valueEs: Solo en el punto de venta final al consumidor

rows.label: Who Collects
rows.value: Only the business selling to the end customer; no input credits
rows.valueEs: Solo el negocio que vende al cliente final; sin créditos fiscales

rows.label: Rate
rows.value: Varies: 0% (Oregon) to 10.25% (some cities). Florida base: 6% + county surtax
rows.valueEs: Varía: 0% (Oregon) a 10.25% (algunas ciudades). Florida base: 6% + sobretasa del condado

rows.label: Included in Price
rows.value: No — tax is added at the register. Shelf price ≠ what you pay
rows.valueEs: No — el impuesto se agrega en la caja. Precio en etiqueta ≠ lo que pagas

rows.label: Filing
rows.value: Separate registration and filing in every state where you have nexus
rows.valueEs: Registro y declaración separados en cada estado donde tengas nexo

rows.label: Example
rows.value: A $100 product in Orlando → you pay $106.50 (6.5% added at register)
rows.valueEs: Un producto de $100 en Orlando → pagas $106.50 (6.5% agregado en caja)

contentBlocks.callout: The biggest shock for international founders: prices on the shelf are NOT what you pay at the register.
contentBlocks.calloutEs: El mayor shock para los fundadores internacionales: los precios en la etiqueta NO son lo que pagas en la caja.
contentBlocks.footer: Florida: 6% base rate + county surtax (0.5%–1.5%). No tax on most services. Exemptions for manufacturing equipment and some food items.
contentBlocks.footerEs: Florida: tasa base del 6% + sobretasa del condado (0.5%–1.5%). Sin impuesto en la mayoría de servicios. Exenciones para equipo de manufactura y algunos alimentos.

contentBlocks.title: Collecting Sales Tax Without a Permit
contentBlocks.content: Here's where founders get it wrong. Some founders start collecting sales tax before registering with the Florida Department of Revenue — that's a compliance violation. Others don't collect at all and absorb the cost themselves — that's a growing liability. The correct sequence: register first, then collect. Registration is free and takes about 10 minutes online. Don't skip this step.

contentBlocks.title: U.S. Bookkeeping Expectations, Why It Matters More Here
contentBlocks.content:
Step across the hall from the compliance bureau to the accounting wing. This is the office in the Financial District that every other building will call — banks want to see your records before lending, the IRS wants them to verify your returns, investors want profit-and-loss statements, and even landlords may review your financials before signing a lease.

The bottom line? The standard is GAAP (Generally Accepted Accounting Principles). For small businesses: separate business and personal finances, record all income and expenses, reconcile bank statements monthly, and keep receipts for at least three years (seven years for some records).

A food truck founder from Peru kept receipts in a shoebox. When his CPA asked for quarterly expense reports, he spent an entire weekend matching paper receipts to bank transactions. QuickBooks from day one would have taken five minutes.

The tools are affordable. QuickBooks Online ($30–$60/month) is one of the most widely used. Xero ($15–$78/month) is popular with international founders for its multi-currency support. Wave is free for basic invoicing and accounting. Pick one before your first sale and connect it to your bank account.

contentBlocks.title: Annual Accounting Costs — What to Budget

rows: QuickBooks Online, Xero, or Wave (free tier available)
Higher end for international clients with treaty filings, FBAR, or multi-state returns
Avalara or TaxJar for multi-state automation
Gusto, ADP Run, or Paychex. Handles withholding and filings.
If you don't want to do it yourself. Bench, Pilot, or local firms.

contentBlocks.title: Bookkeeping Methods — Which One Fits?

sections.heading: Cash Basis Accounting
sections.content:
**How it works:** Record income when you receive payment, expenses when you pay them.

**Best for:** Small businesses with under $25M revenue. Simple and intuitive.

**Watch out:** Can distort your picture if you have large receivables or payables.

sections.heading: Accrual Basis Accounting
sections.content:
**How it works:** Record income when earned and expenses when incurred, regardless of payment.

**Best for:** Businesses seeking investors, applying for loans, or with inventory.

**Watch out:** More complex — usually requires a bookkeeper or accountant.

sections.heading: DIY vs. Outsourced Bookkeeping
sections.content:
**DIY tools:** QuickBooks Online ($30/mo), Wave (free), Xero ($15/mo)

**Outsourced:** Virtual bookkeeper ($200–$500/mo), Local CPA ($300–$800/mo)

**Rule of thumb:** DIY until revenue exceeds $100K/year, then outsource to free your time.

contentBlocks.title: Before You Hire an Accountant
contentBlocks.content:
Think about this: not all CPAs are created equal — and not all of them work with foreign-owned businesses. Before you hire an accountant, ask these questions:

  • Do you have experience filing returns for non-resident aliens or foreign-owned LLCs?
  • Are you familiar with tax treaty provisions for my home country?
  • Can you handle FBAR (Foreign Bank Account Report) filings?
  • Do you charge flat fees or hourly? (Flat fee is almost always better for small businesses.)
  • Can you help with multi-state sales tax compliance?

The SBDC (Small Business Development Center) in Orlando can refer you to CPAs who work with international founders. This is a free service. The Financial District has no shortage of options — Central Florida is home to more than 12,000 accounting and financial services firms, so finding one who can navigate international filings is very achievable.

contentBlocks.title: Business owner researching Florida sales tax requirements on a laptop
What Would You Do?

questions.stem: What is the most important difference between a VAT system and U.S. sales tax for an international founder to understand?
options: VAT rates are always higher than U.S. sales tax rates
U.S. sales tax applies only to services, while VAT applies only to goods
VAT is collected at the point of sale, while U.S. sales tax is collected at manufacturing
U.S. sales tax is managed state-by-state with no federal sales tax, while VAT is typically one national system
questions.explanation: The core difference is structural: VAT is a national, centralized system. U.S. sales tax is decentralized — each state sets its own rules, rates, and exemptions. There is no federal sales tax. VAT rates are not always higher than U.S. rates. Both VAT and U.S. sales tax can apply to goods and services. Both are collected at the point of sale, not at manufacturing. Founders selling in multiple states may need to register and file separately in each one.

questions.stem: A founder from Argentina has been selling leather goods online from Orlando for nine months without registering for a Florida Sales Tax Certificate. What is the most likely consequence?
options: Nothing — sales tax only applies to businesses with over $100,000 in revenue
She will receive a notice from the Florida Department of Revenue for uncollected sales tax plus penalties, which she must pay out of pocket
The IRS will revoke her EIN and close her business
Her customers will be individually liable for the unpaid tax
questions.explanation: The lesson describes this exact scenario: an Argentine founder owed $3,800 in uncollected sales tax plus penalties after nine months. There is no $100,000 revenue threshold for Florida sales tax on tangible goods. The IRS does not revoke EINs for state sales tax violations. Customers are not individually liable — the business bears responsibility for collecting and remitting sales tax. Registration is free and takes about 10 minutes.

questions.stem: A founder from Chile is launching a consulting firm in Orlando. She needs to estimate her first-year accounting costs so she can set a realistic budget. Her business will have one owner, no employees, and clients in Florida only. Which estimate is most realistic?
options: Under $200 total — free bookkeeping software and no need for a CPA since Florida has no income tax
About $500 — just a CPA for annual tax prep, since a single-state consulting firm has minimal complexity
Between $1,000 and $3,000 — bookkeeping software, a CPA experienced with international clients, and potential sales tax filing costs
Over $10,000 — international businesses always require a full-service accounting firm
questions.explanation: A realistic first-year budget includes bookkeeping software ($240-$720/year), a CPA experienced with international clients ($500-$2,500/year), and possibly sales tax filing assistance. Under $200 ignores the need for a CPA who understands non-resident alien filing requirements. A $500 estimate underestimates CPA costs for international clients, who need treaty review and potentially FBAR filings. Over $10,000 is excessive for a small single-owner consulting firm with no employees and one state of operation.

contentBlocks.title: Key Takeaway
contentBlocks.content: The takeaway is simple. Sales tax compliance is mandatory and easy to mess up. Register with the Florida Department of Revenue before collecting tax. U.S. sales tax works nothing like VAT — it's state-by-state with no input credits. Budget for a CPA who understands international clients. The cost of professional accounting ($500–$2,500/year) is far less than the cost of IRS penalties and state compliance violations. Your compliance stop in the Financial District is straightforward if you know the rules before you walk in.

lessons.title: Banking Deep Dive — The Reality for International Founders
objectives: Identify the physical presence and documentation requirements for major U.S. banks
Explain why bank accounts get rejected and how to improve approval chances
Describe U.S. credit history requirements and realistic timelines for building credit

contentBlocks.title: Why Banks Say No
contentBlocks.content:
You have walked past the tax offices and the accounting firms. Now you are standing in front of the tallest building at the end of the Financial District: the bank. This is the stop that trips up more international founders than any other.

You can form your LLC in two days. But getting a bank account? That can take two months and three rejections. Banks reject international founders for specific, predictable reasons:

  • No U.S. credit history. Home-country credit does not transfer. A German founder with a 15-year Deutsche Bank relationship was turned away by Chase because he had no U.S. credit score.
  • Missing documents. Every bank has a different checklist, and most do not post it clearly. Walk in missing one document and you will come back another day.
  • Entity type mismatch. Some banks prefer C-Corps over single-member LLCs. Others will not open accounts for entities less than 30 days old.
  • High-risk country flags. Anti-money-laundering rules mean founders from certain countries face added review or refusal.

contentBlocks.title: Founder Facing Bank Rejection
contentBlocks.alt: Illustration of a founder standing outside a bank, contemplating next steps after a rejection.

contentBlocks.title: What You Can Do About It
contentBlocks.content:
A Kenyan fintech founder applied at three banks, each asking for more KYC documents: proof of where her funds came from, notarized business records, a letter from her Kenyan bank. She eventually succeeded at a regional Orlando bank experienced with African founders, but the process took seven weeks.

International-friendly banks (early 2026): [Mercury](https://mercury.com), [Relay](https://relayfi.com), Centennial Bank, and Valley National Bank have been more willing to work with international founders. Chase and Bank of America have stricter non-resident requirements. Always call ahead and confirm before visiting a branch.

Important: Banking policies are not uniform across institutions, and they change frequently. What worked for another founder six months ago may not apply to your situation today. Call ahead, ask specific questions, and get answers in writing when possible.

contentBlocks.title: The Bank Said No — What's Your Next Move?
contentBlocks.description: You applied for a business checking account at a major national bank. After two weeks of waiting, you received an email: 'We are unable to open an account at this time.' No explanation. What do you do?

paths.label: Apply at a different national bank immediately
paths.outcome: Risky approach. If the rejection was due to missing documentation or KYC flags, the same issues will follow you to the next bank. Before reapplying anywhere, find out WHY you were rejected.

paths.label: Call the bank and ask specifically why you were rejected
paths.outcome: Smart first step. Banks are not always required to explain, but many will give you a general reason — missing documents, entity age, or country-of-origin review requirements. This information tells you what to fix before your next application.

paths.label: Switch to a payment processor like Stripe or [Mercury](https://mercury.com) while you sort it out
paths.outcome: Practical and common. Many international founders use [Mercury](https://mercury.com) or [Relay](https://relayfi.com) as their primary account while building U.S. banking history. These fintech platforms have more flexible requirements for non-residents. You can always add a traditional bank later.

contentBlocks.title: Reality Check: This Takes Longer Than You Think
contentBlocks.content:
Here's the thing. First bank account: 2 to 4 weeks if everything goes right. Some founders report 6 to 8 weeks with additional compliance review.

Business credit card: 3–6 months after account opening. Most require a U.S. credit score or a secured deposit.

Business credit line: 12–18 months of banking history and revenue documentation.

Plan your cash flow around these timelines. Don't expect to walk into a bank on Monday and have an account by Friday.

contentBlocks.title: Building U.S. Credit History From Zero
contentBlocks.content:
Building U.S. credit is like establishing your reputation in the Financial District. You start as a stranger — nobody knows you. Every successful transaction, every on-time payment, adds to your standing. Your U.S. credit score starts at nothing, not low, but truly absent. Many systems reject requests when no score exists. The good news is that the Orlando metro has over 400 bank branches and credit union locations, so once you have your documents in order, options are plentiful. Here's the path to build credit:

Here is the practical takeaway. Step 1: Secured credit card. Deposit $500–$2,000 as collateral, use it for small purchases, pay in full monthly. After 6–12 months, you'll have a basic score. Capital One, Discover, and some credit unions offer secured cards to non-residents with an ITIN.

Step 2: Business credit card (not requiring personal credit). Brex and Ramp use your EIN and business revenue instead of personal credit. [Mercury](https://mercury.com) offers a business card connected to your [Mercury](https://mercury.com) account.

Step 3: Credit-builder accounts. Services like Self or Grow Credit report monthly payments to credit bureaus, building your score without a traditional credit card.

Credit affects more than banking. It shapes lease terms, vendor payment terms (Net 30/Net 60), and access to loans. A Peruvian founder paid three months' rent upfront ($6,000 in tied-up capital) because she had no U.S. credit history. Start building credit the day you open your bank account.

contentBlocks.title: Payment Processors, Wire Transfers, and Moving Money
contentBlocks.content:
Before you leave the Financial District, stop at the service counter near the exit. Payment processors are like the district's quick-service windows — they handle transactions while you work on getting full access to the big buildings. While you work on getting a bank account, you still need to accept payments and move money.

Stripe: Requires a U.S. bank account for payouts. Fastest way to accept credit card payments online. Processing fee: 2.9% + $0.30 per transaction.

The bottom line? PayPal: Set up with an EIN and U.S. address. Useful as an interim solution, but known for freezing accounts with sudden transaction spikes. A Brazilian founder had $12,000 frozen for 21 days after a product launch triggered fraud detection.

Wise (formerly TransferWise): Gives you a U.S. account number and routing number for ACH payments. Not a full bank replacement, but excellent for moving money between countries at favorable exchange rates.

In other words, choose the right tool for the job. ACH vs. Wire vs. Check: ACH is the U.S. standard for recurring payments — free or low-cost, 1–3 business days. Wire transfers are for urgent or international payments ($25–$50 per transfer). Most B2B payments are ACH or credit card.

International wire costs add up fast. Compare your bank ($30–$50 fee + 2–3% exchange markup) against Wise ($5–$10 fee + mid-market rate) — the difference can save hundreds per month.

contentBlocks.title: Watch Your Money — Wire Transfer Fees Are Real
contentBlocks.content: Here's where it adds up. International wires cost $25 to $50 each direction through traditional banks, plus unfavorable exchange rates that add another 1 to 3%. ACH transfers are free or low-cost but domestic only. Plan your money movement strategy: batch international transfers to reduce fees, use Wise or similar services for better rates, and switch to ACH for domestic payments as soon as your U.S. bank account is active.

contentBlocks.title: Banking Terminology — Key Terms for International Founders

cards.back: Automated Clearing House — electronic bank-to-bank transfer. Takes 1–3 business days. Low or no fees.
Same-day bank transfer for large amounts. Costs $15–$30 domestic, $35–$50 international.
Individual Taxpayer Identification Number — IRS-issued for non-residents who can't get an SSN.
Know Your Customer — bank verification process requiring ID, proof of business, and source of funds.
Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation — protects deposits up to $250,000 per account.
Customer disputes a card transaction. Merchant must prove the charge was legitimate or refund it.

contentBlocks.title: A Quick Word About Funding and Investors
contentBlocks.content:
Standing at the edge of the Financial District, you can see the investor tower in the distance. Most early-stage founders are not ready to walk in there yet. Here is a question every founder asks: can you raise money from U.S. investors or get a loan? Yes, but not as easily as you might hope. Most early-stage investors want to see traction : paying customers, revenue growth, or strong user metrics — before writing a check. An idea and a pitch deck, no matter how good, rarely gets funded without evidence of U.S. market demand.

The bottom line? That's why most international founders bootstrap their first 6–12 months using personal savings, home-country revenue, or small investments from friends and family. Build traction first, then approach investors.

Why does this matter? The Small Business Administration (SBA) offers popular loan programs, but most require the borrower to be a U.S. citizen or green card holder. Non-citizen founders often don't qualify, even with a strong Florida business. Some CDFIs and microlenders have fewer restrictions, but the amounts are smaller.

We cover funding pathways in more depth in phases 2 and 3 of the [UCF BIP](https://incubator.ucf.edu) market entry/soft landing program. Plan to fund your U.S. entry yourself for the first year.

contentBlocks.title: Latin America Resources — Banking
contentBlocks.content: Here's the key insight for Latin American founders. Central Florida has banking resources tailored for you. Several banks in the Orlando area have Spanish-speaking staff and are accustomed to working with international clients. The [Hispanic Chamber of Commerce of Metro Orlando](https://hispanicchamber.com/) hosts banking workshops that cover account setup and credit building. The [SBDC at UCF](https://sbdcorlando.com/) offers free one-on-one financial counseling sessions where you can get guidance on banking, accounting, and funding options specific to your situation.

contentBlocks.title: What Would You Do?

questions.stem: An international founder with no U.S. credit history wants to start building credit immediately after opening a bank account. What is the recommended first step?
options: Apply for a business line of credit from a major bank
Ask a U.S. friend to add them as an authorized user on their credit card
Open a secured credit card with a cash deposit as collateral
Wait 12 months and then apply for a standard credit card
questions.explanation: A secured credit card is the standard first step for building U.S. credit from zero. You deposit cash as collateral and use the card for small purchases, paying in full each month. Applying for a business line of credit requires an existing credit history that new founders do not have. Relying on a friend's account does not build independent credit history in your name. Waiting 12 months delays credit building when you could start immediately.

questions.stem: A Peruvian founder just arrived in Orlando and wants to sign a commercial lease for office space. Her landlord is asking for a larger security deposit because she has no U.S. credit history. What should she have done earlier to avoid this situation?
options: Applied for an SBA loan to demonstrate financial stability
Opened a secured credit card immediately after getting her bank account and used it consistently for 6-12 months
Asked her home-country bank to transfer her credit score to a U.S. bureau
Paid the landlord in cash upfront to bypass the credit check entirely
questions.explanation: Starting with a secured credit card the day you open your bank account builds a basic credit score within 6-12 months. SBA loans typically require U.S. citizenship or a green card, so most international founders do not qualify. Home-country credit scores do not transfer to U.S. credit bureaus. Paying cash upfront ties up capital and does not solve the underlying problem of having no U.S. credit history for future transactions.

questions.stem: A founder from Nigeria has registered her Florida LLC and received her EIN. She plans to visit a Chase branch in Orlando next week to open a business bank account. She has her passport and EIN letter ready. Based on what this lesson covers about documentation and physical presence, what should she do before walking into the branch?
options: Nothing else is needed — a passport and EIN letter are sufficient for any U.S. bank
She should wait until she has a U.S. Social Security Number before attempting to open an account
She should bring her Articles of Organization but skip calling ahead since all branches handle international accounts the same way

She should call Chase first to confirm their specific requirements for foreign-owned entities, and bring her Articles of Organization, operating agreement, and proof of U.S. address in addition to her passport and EIN letter
questions.explanation: Banks have different documentation requirements, and not every branch handles foreign-owned accounts. Calling ahead to confirm what Chase specifically needs for her situation prevents wasted trips. A passport and EIN alone are rarely sufficient — most banks also require Articles of Organization, an operating agreement, and proof of a U.S. address. Waiting for an SSN is unnecessary since many banks accept an EIN alone, but she must verify this with the specific branch. Assuming all branches work the same way ignores the lesson's emphasis that branch-level familiarity with international accounts varies widely.

contentBlocks.title: Key Takeaway
contentBlocks.content: Banking is one of the biggest pain points for founders. Come to every bank visit with all your documents, apply to banks that welcome foreign owners, and start building U.S. credit from day one with a secured credit card. Plan to fund yourself the first year. Investors and SBA loans have rules that take time to meet. Needs vary by bank and change often, so always check before you apply. The Financial District does not hand out trust freely — you earn it one transaction at a time.

moduleLearningOutcomes: Explain federal and state tax obligations for foreign-owned U.S. entities
Describe sales tax collection requirements and basic accounting/bookkeeping expectations
Navigate the full banking process including document requirements, common rejections, credit history, and realistic timelines
